The Karan Gupta

Poet · Researcher · Builder · Founder

Vol. I  ·  No. 26  ·  Tuesday, February 24, 2026  ·  San Francisco, CA

HOME | WORK | POETRY | COMMONPLACE | ABOUT | CONTACT
The Commonplace Book

A Constellation of Ideas

A living archive of quotes, annotations, and intellectual cross-pollination — the compost pile that feeds everything else. Entries are connected by semantic similarity; the constellation view visualizes those relationships.

16 entries · Toggle to constellation view to see connections

"The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in the mind at the same time, and still retain the ability to function."

— F. Scott Fitzgerald

The Crack-Up, 1936

I return to this constantly when I feel the tension between making art and making things that work. The capacity to hold contradiction without resolving it prematurely — this seems central to both poetic and engineering thinking.

"A city is not gauged by its length and width, but by the broadness of its vision and the height of its dreams."

— Herb Caen

Baghdad-by-the-Bay, 1949

Herb Caen was San Francisco's poet laureate before the role existed — his daily column the city's unofficial diary for forty years. I think about this when I watch the skyline change and wonder what we're dreaming now.

"Play is the highest form of research."

— Albert Einstein

Attributed

The whole premise of PlayKits. Also a useful antidote to the utilitarian pressure that follows every young person who shows aptitude for something. Not everything worth doing is worth monetizing.

"We are the stories we tell ourselves."

— Joan Didion

The White Album, 1979

And also the stories others tell about us, and the stories we refuse. Identity is narrative — partial, revised, subject to new evidence. I find this liberating rather than destabilizing.

"The brain is wider than the sky."

— Emily Dickinson

Poems (Fr598), 1862

Working at Neuroscape, I keep this poem nearby. We map the brain with electrodes and fMRI and still the thing eludes us. Dickinson got there first with sixteen words.

"In the beginner's mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert's mind there are few."

— Shunryu Suzuki

Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind, 1970

The danger of competence: certainty calcifies into blindness. I try to carry beginners' mind into every new project — to ask the naive question that the experts stopped asking years ago.

"Poetry is the clear expression of mixed feelings."

— W.H. Auden

The Dyer's Hand, 1962

What I reach for when I explain poetry to someone who thinks they don't like it. Mixed feelings are the most honest kind. Clarity about ambivalence — this is the work.

"The medium is the message."

— Marshall McLuhan

Understanding Media, 1964

I think about this in the context of the attention economy: the format of social media doesn't just carry content, it shapes cognition. TikTok isn't neutral. Neither is the printed broadsheet. Neither is this website.

"There is no greater agony than bearing an untold story inside you."

— Maya Angelou

I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings, 1969

This is why the poetry workshops matter. Not to produce poets — to give people permission to say what they already know. The story wants to be told. It just needs the right room.

"Technology is neither good nor bad; nor is it neutral."

— Melvin Kranzberg

Technology and History: "Kranzberg's Laws", 1986

Kranzberg's first law. What I return to when anyone tries to tell me that code is just code, that the algorithm is just math. Context is everything. Intent is not enough. We must study effects.

"Wherever you are is called Here, and you must treat it as a powerful stranger."

— David Wagoner

Lost, 1978

A poem about getting lost in the forest that is also about getting lost everywhere else. I read this before every new project, every new city, every time I'm not sure what's next.

"I am not afraid of storms, for I am learning how to sail my ship."

— Louisa May Alcott

Little Women, 1869

Jo March said this and I believed her. Still do. The ship is under-construction and the storms are constant but the learning is real.

"Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity."

— Simone Weil

Waiting for God, 1951

Weil was writing about prayer, but also about teaching, about love, about presence. To really attend to someone — not to process them, not to prepare your response, but to actually look — this is radical.

"The purpose of art is to lay bare the questions that have been hidden by the answers."

— James Baldwin

The Creative Process, 1962

Baldwin again and again. I keep coming back to this because it's also the purpose of good engineering, good science, good teaching. The answer is rarely the end of the conversation.

"I am not what happened to me, I am what I choose to become."

— Carl Gustav Jung

Attributed

Agency as practice, not given. You have to choose it daily. Sometimes hourly. This is hard to explain to people who think identity is just assigned at birth.

"What we build, builds us."

— Karan Gupta

PlayKits Founding Statement, 2022

I wrote this as the organizing principle for PlayKits and it keeps turning out to be true in every context. The systems we design, the tools we build, the habits we form — they shape us as surely as we shape them.

The Karan Gupta

Poet · Researcher · Builder · Founder

San Francisco, California
